$AAOI $COHR $CSCO $LITE A 6.4T  engine uses more Indium Phosphide (InP) materials than a 3.2T engine.Why 6.4T Consumes More InP MaterialsThe primary driver behind InP material consumption is the total lane count and light source volume required to double the bandwidth from 3.2 T per second  to 6.4 Tbps:Lane Count Multiplication: To scale up to multi-terabit architectures, optical engines rely on multiplying physical lanes or laser wavelengths. A 6.4T engine requires twice the data-carrying capacity of a 3.2T engine. Even when transitioning to next-generation individual 400G-per-lane speeds, a 6.4T engine requires 16 channels, whereas a 3.2T engine requires only 8 channels.Laser Component Volume: Whether the architecture uses discrete InP Electro-absorption Modulated Lasers (EMLs) or continuous-wave (CW) InP lasers arrayed to feed a Silicon Photonics (SiPho) engine, the physical number of laser chips or array sizes must scale up to deliver the necessary optical power and channels.

Markets have been following a clear trajectory since the launch of ChatGPT and AI in late 2022, and that’s rotating themes.

The Artificial Intelligence technological renaissance is the high level theme, driving a major bull market that likely lasts many more years.

But within that, we have seen many sub themes come and go.

We had NVDA lead the initial wave with compute and GPUs… obvious in hindsight that we needed immense amounts of compute to train these LLMs.

Then came the power wave as we realized data centers needed immense electricity, names like CEG and VST soared, while spec names like OKLO and now BE followed suit. 

Then came the massive memory crunch, $MU, $SNDK and more got bid to the moon and keep going higher.

Photonics has been a major theme in recent months with the likes of $COHR  $AXTI and $LITE . 

The next theme is bigger.

Bringing AI to the physical world.

The Products: They manufacture foundational substrate wafers including Indium Phosphide (InP), Gallium Arsenide (GaAs), and Germanium (Ge).Vertical Integration: Through its Chinese subsidiary Beijing Tongmei, AXTI owns the entire supply chain. They extract the raw materials, refine them to extreme purities, and synthesize them into single crystals.Extreme Purity: They process standard 4N (99.99%) gallium into ultra-high purity 5N, 6N (99.9999%), up to 8N purity.Market Position: They control roughly 30% to 40% of the global supply for Indium Phosphide substrates, which is the critical material used to generate lasers for modern AI infrastructure and optical interconnects.

Photonics: $AAOI $SIVE $AXTI $LITE $COHR $GLW  
 
The AI infrastructure story is far from over, and photonics is becoming an increasingly important piece of the puzzle. As AI training and inference workloads continue to grow, data movement—not compute itself—is emerging as the next major bottleneck. 
 
AAOI, LITE, and COHR benefit from rising demand for optical modules, lasers, and high-speed interconnects. SIVE is positioned around silicon photonics, a technology that could reshape next-generation data centers. AXTI supplies critical semiconductor materials, while GLW remains a key player in fiber optics and specialty glass. 
 
Investors spent years focusing on GPUs. The next phase may be about moving data faster and more efficiently. If AI spending continues to expand, the photonics ecosystem could become one of the most important growth themes of the next decade. 
 
Which name do you think has the biggest upside from here: AAOI, LITE, or SIVE?

$NOK just dropped a meaningful structural signal for the optical / AI infra chain. 
 
A 10x expansion of its photonic chip facility in Allentown, PA with capacity targeted online by Q3 2026 isn’t just incremental—it’s a step-function in US-based optical manufacturing exposure. With <2% of advanced semiconductor test/packaging currently onshore, this puts Nokia into a very small strategic cohort tied directly to AI datacenter buildouts. 
 
Yet the market is still valuing it like a legacy telecom name at ~3.4x forward revenue, while peers like $CIEN (~11.0x) and $COHR (~11.6x) are already priced as AI optical beneficiaries serving the same end customers. 
 
The disconnect is largely mix-based—optical is still a smaller % of $NOK revenue-but capacity expansion + SOTP logic starts to re-rate that mix over time. 
 
This is how optical exposure gets repriced—quietly, then all at once.

Optical / photonics names are basically doing what strong trend sectors usually do after a big move-cool off and digest gains instead of reversing the whole structure. 
 
What stands out here is that the broader AI infrastructure buildout story hasn’t changed, even if short-term momentum has paused. These are still core beneficiaries of rising data center bandwidth demand, just in a consolidation phase rather than expansion mode. 
 
Historically, when a sector like this pauses after a strong run, it’s often less about “end of trend” and more about resetting positioning before the next leg-assuming demand trends stay intact. 
 
Worth keeping on radar rather than writing off. Trends don’t end quickly when the underlying capex cycle is still active.

$AAOI / $LITE / $COHR / $FN 
I’ve been watching names like $AAOI, $LITE, $COHR, and $FN after that strong run, and honestly this looks more like a pause than anything else. 
 
After big vertical moves, I’ve learned these don’t usually just roll over immediately  they tend to go quiet first, kind of shake out attention, then decide on the next leg later. 
 
Right now it just feels like: 
momentum cooled off a bit 
price is chopping sideways instead of trending 
volume normalizing after the spike phase 
no real breakdown, just digestion 
 
And that’s usually where people get bored and move on…  
which is often the part I don’t want to ignore. 
 
Not chasing anything here, just keeping them on the radar. Trends like this in optics / photonics tied to AI infra don’t usually end clean  they pause, reset, then reprice when no one is looking.

INP SUBSTRATES - QUICK CLARIFICATION THREAD 
 
A few important points on the InP supply chain: 
 
• $AXTI is a key upstream player in InP substrates 
- has been getting permits to ship to U.S. companies operating outside the U.S. 
- still a critical “gate” supplier in the ecosystem 
 
• $COHR  - works with ~5 substrate suppliers (not single-sourced) 
                  - diversification is preferred for visibility + risk control 
 
• $LITE   - has long-term supply secured via Sumitomo 
               - still values multi-source optionality 
 
• $LITE / $COHR / $AAOI / $NOK - do NOT manufacture InP substrates 
   - they source substrates (AXT / others) and then build devices 
 
• Key distinction: 
“InP production / capacity” ≠ “InP substrates” 
 
- Device makers expand lasers, photonics, modulators, etc. 
- Substrate makers actually grow the raw material wafer supply 
 
The bottleneck sits upstream in substrates, while most headlines refer to downstream device expansion.

$ADTN  Thesis:

FOR context, $LITE trades at 25x forward P/S and $COHR trades at 9x. Both are in the exact same optical interconnect space. ADTN is 1/60th the size of either of them with a better product on the efficiency curve.

AI data centers are hitting a hard wall on power and thermal density. The industry is migrating fast from DSP-based transceivers to Linear Pluggable Optics, an architecture that removes the digital signal processor entirely, cutting latency, power draw and cost per bit simultaneously. 

ADTN built directly to that spec. On March 10 they launched LiteWave800, an 800G LPO module running on single-mode VCSEL architecture at 1 pJ/bit energy efficiency. That is 6 to 10x better than first generation LPO and 12 to 18x better than traditional DSP transceivers. Goldman’s report specifically calls out VCSEL as the high efficiency commercially ready light source for exactly these applications.

$IQEPF Why $IQEPF Matters
As data demands continue to rise, optical interconnects are increasingly replacing traditional copper-based systems, particularly at higher speeds (e.g., 800G transitioning toward 1.6T). This trend could drive sustained demand for photonic components and, by extension, the materials that enable them.

Because $IQEPF sits upstream in the value chain, it may benefit from this shift without being directly tied to any single end-product company.

Additionally, $IQEPF supplies key players in the space, including companies like $LITE, which rely on high-quality epitaxial wafers for their components.

Competitive Positioning
While $IQEPF is not the only company in this space, relatively few firms specialize in advanced epitaxial wafer manufacturing at scale. This creates a potentially favorable competitive position, though not without competition from players like $COHR and others.

Yes, you are missing the entire concept of corporate risk-isolation and R&D off-boarding.

KOPN buys a 20% non-dilutive stake in SBLX. KOPN gets paid $15M to build the prototype. SBLX takes the commercial/DC market rights, while KOPN keeps the defense market rights.

Why this is a highly lethal, mathematically sound structure for KOPN...

KOPN is a defense contractor at its core. They build displays for F-35 helmets and thermal sights. They kept 100% of the rights to the sector they already dominate. They didn't risk their baseline.

KOPN does not have the billions of dollars in marketing capital required to walk into Microsoft or Google and displace legacy optical monopolies like $COHR or $LITE. If they tried to commercialize this themselves, they would bankrupt the company.

By spinning the commercial rights out to SBLX, KOPN is forcing SBLX to take all the commercialization risk. SBLX has to raise the money. SBLX has to hire the sales team. SBLX has to pitch the HPCs.

One of Wall Street’s biggest AI debates—copper vs. optical networking—may be missing the bigger picture, as both technologies are positioned to benefit from the data center boom. 
According to Mizuho, demand for AI infrastructure will support both copper-based and optical components through at least 2029. Copper remains cost-effective and efficient for short-distance, high-density connections, while optical solutions are better suited for longer distances and higher bandwidth needs. 
This balanced view aligns with comments from NVIDIA Corporation CEO Jensen Huang, who emphasized that scaling AI systems will require a mix of networking technologies rather than a single winner. 
Companies tied to copper connectivity like Credo Technology Group and optical players such as Lumentum Holdings and Coherent Corp. are all seen as beneficiaries of the trend. 
Overall, the AI networking buildout is large enough that both copper and optical ecosystems can win.

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $IONQ Hybrid Integration (The Real Secret): TFLN doesn't actually have to completely replace Silicon Photonics. Through a process called wafer-level bonding, manufacturers can literally glue a microscopic thin film of Lithium Niobate directly on top of a standard, cheap silicon wafer. This means you get the ultra-cheap manufacturing routing of silicon, combined with the ultra-fast modulation engine of TFLN.

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $IONQ Is It Cost-Effective?
​Historically, no. Lithium Niobate used to be considered a cottage industry material. Hard to work with, brittle, and expensive to produce on tiny 4-inch or 6-inch wafers.  
​However, two massive manufacturing breakthroughs have recently made it cost-effective for commercial use:
​The 8-Inch Wafer Shift: As of 2025/2026, the industry has cracked the code on manufacturing TFLN on 8-inch (200mm) wafers. Scaling up the wafer size drastically increases throughput and drops the per-die cost, making it commercially viable against legacy materials.

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $IONQ Extreme Energy Efficiency. Heat & power consumption are the biggest bottlenecks in AI data centers right now. Traditional modulators require power-hungry external amplifiers to drive the signal. TFLN has extremely low optical loss and a very low "drive voltage" (often under 2 volts, sometimes sub-1 volt). This means TFLN modulators can be driven directly by standard electronics without those extra amplifiers, drastically cutting power consumption & heat.

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $IONQ Unmatched Speed. Standard Silicon or InP modulators hit physical limitations around an optical bandwidth of 30 GHz to 40 GHz. TFLN modulators inherently possess an ultra-fast electro-optic effect (the Pockels effect). They routinely exceed 100 GHz and are hitting up to 220 GHz in pilot lines. This is the difference between struggling to hit 800 Gigabits per second and easily cruising at 1.6 or 3.2 Terabits per second.

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $IONQ  To move data using light, you need a modulator. The component that translates electrical signals into optical signals. Silicon & Indium Phosphide (InP) have done this well for years, but TFLN blows them out of the water in two critical metrics

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $IONQ  QUBT is betting that their TFLN chips will eventually replace or heavily supplement those older materials because TFLN offers better power efficiency and lower latency.

$IONQ $QUBT $RGTI  $LITE $COHR  The advantage is their (Qubt) Dirac-3 system doesn't require the extreme sub-zero cooling or vacuum chambers that IonQ or Rigetti need. It runs at room temperature using light photons. This makes the hardware much cheaper to build & easier to put in a standard data center rack.

$QUBT $LITE $COHR $FN $QBTS  Unlike other quantum companies that rely on third-party foundries, QUBT is attempting to become a vertically integrated manufacturer. Why AEHR is super important with Optics Photonic industry:

Aehr Test Systems plays a surprisingly important—but often overlooked—role in the optical/photonics supply chain, especially as data centers, AI, and high-speed networking explode

This matters because:
 • Photonics chips are very sensitive to defects
 • Failures in optical networks (data centers, telecom) are catastrophic and expensive

So companies need to:
 • Detect early-life failures
 • Ensure long-term reliability

Aehr’s systems (like FOX platform) do exactly that.

Aehr is important to optics/photonics because:
 • Photonics chips are still semiconductor devices needing rigorous testing
 • Reliability is mission-critical in AI + data centers
 • AEHR provides wafer-level burn-in, which:
 • Improves yield
 • Prevents failures
 • Enables scaling

As optical interconnects replace copper in AI infrastructure, AEHR quietly becomes a critical enabler behind the scenes

 • yields drop
 • costs rise
 • reliability risks increase
 • adoption slows

 • Gatekeeper of reliability

Silicon photonics = high-performance race cars
Aehr = stress-testing every engine before the race

As silicon photonics scales (especially with AI),
AEHR becomes more valuable—not less

$COHR Coherent to unveil AI-scale optical innovations at OFC 2026 
 
Coherent will showcase breakthrough innovations powering the next generation of AI-driven datacenter and communications networks at OFC 2026, March 17 -19, at the L.A. Convention Center in Los Angeles, California, Booth #1401. From 400G/lane, 3.2T transceivers and emerging architectures for 12.8T and beyond, to advanced co-packaged optics, multi-rail transport, and open optical networking platforms, Coherent will demonstrate how its vertical technology stack - spanning materials, devices, modules, and systems - is redefining performance, scalability, and energy efficiency for the AI era.  
 
"At OFC 2026, we are demonstrating how Coherent's innovation engines, from advanced materials to fully integrated optical systems - are enabling the infrastructure backbone of AI," said Dr. Sanjai Parthasarathi, CMO at Coherent. "Our technologies are driving the bandwidth, power efficiency, and scalability required for the next decade of optical networking." In addition, Coherent executives and technology leaders will take center stage across plenaries, executive forums, panels, and technical sessions, helping shape the future roadmap of optical networking.

$COHR Coherent demonstrates co-packaged optics tech at OFC 2026 
 
Coherent will demonstrate multiple co-packaged optics technologies at OFC 2026 in Los Angeles, highlighting the company's broad portfolio and vertical technology stack capabilities to support the rapidly growing bandwidth demands of AI and high-performance computing infrastructure. Coherent will showcase several CPO approaches designed to enable next-generation data center architectures. Demonstrations include a 6.4T socketed CPO based on silicon photonics, paired with Coherent's External Laser Source module powered by its own high-power InP CW lasers; a multimode socketed CPO built with Coherent's high-speed VCSELs; and an InP modulator on silicon operating at 400G.  
 
The InP-based demonstration features a 400G-per-lane InP modulator array, illustrating a pathway toward higher lane speeds and the scalability required for future CPO architectures. These demonstrations highlight Coherent's ability to support multiple optical architectures for co-packaged optics, leveraging its expertise across key photonics technologies including indium phosphide lasers, silicon photonics, VCSELs, and advanced packaging.

$COHR Coherent to showcase pluggable optical tech portfolio at OFC 2026 
 
Coherent Corp. will showcase a comprehensive portfolio of next-generation pluggable optical technologies at OFC 2026, spanning 1.6T, 3.2T, and emerging architectures for 12.8T and beyond. The demonstrations highlight Coherent's multi-technology platform strategy - encompassing Silicon Photonics, Indium Phosphide, and VCSEL-based solutions - designed to enable scalable, power-efficient connectivity for AI-driven data center infrastructure. Coherent will also present multiple 1.6T transceivers, featuring different optical technologies such as: Silicon Photonics Photonic Integrated Circuit, high power InP CW laser, 200G InP EML, and 200G GaAs VCSEL. The demonstrations will include three different DSP solutions from three industry leaders, as well as multiple types of electrical interfaces.  
 
Together, these demonstrations underscore Coherent's ability to execute across diverse technology platforms. For emerging 3.2T transceivers, Coherent will demonstrate 400G/lane PAM4 optical links with both 400G Differential EML as well as a silicon photonics PIC implementation based on Coherent's 400G pure silicon PN junction Mach-Zender Modulator. These high-speed link demonstrations validate Coherent's ability to lead next-generation 3.2T pluggable architectures. Looking further ahead to 12.8T and beyond, Coherent will also demonstrate a new multi-lane XPO pluggable MSA form factor, designed to enhance system design agility while optimizing power and performance.

1. “10b5-1 well in advance 50k or however many shares…”

This refers to a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan under the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

A 10b5-1 plan lets insiders (CEO, directors, etc.) schedule stock sales in advance when they don’t have material non-public information.

What the poster is arguing:
 • Someone at Aeluma sold 50,000 shares.
 • But the sale was pre-scheduled under a 10b5-1 plan.
 • Therefore it shouldn’t be interpreted as insider dumping or loss of confidence.

Recent momentum was amplified by Nvidia’s multibillion-dollar optical deals with Coherent  and Lumentum Holdings, highlighting strong sector demand. AOI previously missed 800G revenue expectations due to a firmware issue affecting interoperability with Amazon. com , but management remains bullish.

Aeluma QD Laser Advantages over Traditional EMLs
 • Monolithic Integration & Scale:Unlike traditional EMLs that often require separate packaging and fiber coupling (hybrid assembly), Aeluma directly grows III-V materials (InGaAs/GaAs) on silicon, enabling on-chip, mass-market manufacturing.
 • Lower Cost & Energy Efficiency:The use of 300mm silicon substrates allows for scalable, cost-effective manufacturing. Having the laser source on-chip improves energy efficiency and reduces the footprint, crucial for next-generation AI infrastructure.
 • Superior Performance in Harsh Conditions: Quantum dot lasers are more temperature-stable, meaning they can operate at high temperatures (e.g., 85°C) without the heavy cooling required by traditional lasers, which is vital for co-packaged optics (CPO).
 • Feedback Insensitivity: QD lasers often do not require an optical isolator, simplifying the assembly, reducing components, and lowering the bill of materials.

Coherent Corp. 
(COHR) has demonstrated a robust performance recently, primarily driven by its strategic movements in the photonics sector and the accelerating demand for AI-driven data center solutions. 
The company's decision to divest its Materials Processing Tools Product Division to Bystronic is a pivotal step aimed at reducing debt, which is projected to enhance earnings per share (EPS) immediately.

Recent product launches, such as next-generation polarization-maintaining optical fibers and advanced optical transceivers, position Coherent favorably against industry peers, enhancing its competitive edge. 
The company’s commitment to innovation is underscored by its recognition at the ECOC 2025 for optical innovation, further solidifying its market leadership. 
In terms of financial metrics, Coherent's revenue forecasts are promising, with analysts projecting continued growth driven by the burgeoning AI infrastructure market. 
The company has seen a significant appreciation in its stock price, climbing 102% over the past six months, which highlights the positive market sentiment surrounding its initiatives in AI and photonics technology. 
Comparatively, peers in the semiconductor and photonics sectors are also experiencing growth, but Coherent's unique positioning in the AI-driven data center space sets it apart. 
Looking ahead, Coherent is scheduled to release its Q1 earnings on November 5, 2025. 
Analysts expect strong results, with the consensus estimates suggesting a significant EPS increase. 
Historically, the company has performed well in its earnings reports, often exceeding analyst expectations, which could lead to further upward momentum in its stock price. 
The upcoming earnings release is crucial, as it will not only reflect the company’s operational performance but also provide insights into its strategic direction in light of current market conditions. 
Overall, the photonics sector, to which Coherent belongs, has shown resilience and growth potential, particularly as demand for advanced optical technologies continues to rise in tandem with the expansion of AI and data center capabilities. 
The sector's positive outlook, combined with Coherent's innovative product offerings and strategic initiatives, bodes well for its future performance.
